title: Contributing
text: Fork package clone onto computer. haven’t done , recommend using usethis::create_from_github("tesselle/PACKAGE", fork = TRUE). Install development dependencies devtools::install_dev_deps(), make sure package passes R CMD check running devtools::check(). R CMD check doesn’t pass cleanly, ’s good idea ask help continuing. Create Git branch pull request (PR). recommend using usethis::pr_init("brief-description--change"). Make changes, commit git, create PR running usethis::pr_push(), following prompts browser. title PR briefly describe change. body PR contain Fixes #issue-number. user-facing changes, add bullet top NEWS.md (.e. just first header). Follow style described https://documentation.tesselle.org.

Refer original document details recommended uses (see references).

title: Paul Tol's Colour Schemes
text: general rule, ordered data represented using rainbow scheme. three main arguments use (Tol 2021): spectral order visible light carries inherent magnitude message. bands almost constant hue sharp transitions , can perceived jumps data. Colour-blind people difficulty distinguishing colours rainbow. use avoided, Paul Tol’s technical note provides two colour schemes reasonably clear colour-blind vision. remain colour-blind safe, two schemes must comply following conditions: discrete rainbow scheme must interpolated. smooth rainbow scheme used full range (Tol 2021 suggests starting purple).  using smooth rainbow scheme: Start -white instead purple lowest data value occurs often; End red instead brown highest data value occurs often.
code: discrete_rainbow <- colour("discrete rainbow") plot_scheme(discrete_rainbow(14), colours = TRUE, size = 0.7) smooth_rainbow <- colour("smooth rainbow")  # Start at purple instead of off-white plot(smooth_rainbow(256, range = c(0.25, 1))) # End at red instead of brown plot(smooth_rainbow(256, range = c(0, 0.9)))

title: Colour Schemes for Scientific Data Visualization
text: Color blindness affects large number individuals. communicating scientific results colour palettes must therefore carefully chosen accessible readers. R package provides implementation Okabe Ito[1], Paul Tol[2] Fabio Crameri[3] colour schemes. schemes ready type data (qualitative, diverging sequential), colours distinct people, including colour-blind readers. package also provides tools simulate colour-blindness test well colours palette identifiable. simulate colour-blindness production-ready R figures may also interested colorblindr package. specific uses, several scientific thematic schemes (geologic timescale, land cover, FAO soils, etc.) implemented, colour schemes may colour-blind safe. colour schemes implemented use base R ggplot2.

text: colour() returns palette function called single integer argument returns vector colours. crayon installed machine crayon.enabled option set TRUE options(), colours nicely printed console.  can disable feature setting crayon.enabled option FALSE.

title: Color Palette — colour
text: palette function following attributes, called single integer argument (number levels) returns (named) vector colors. palette character string giving name color scheme. type character string giving corresponding data type. One "qualitative", "diverging" "sequential". interpolate logical scalar: can color palette interpolated? missing character string giving hexadecimal representation color used NA values. max integer giving maximum number color values. relevant non-interpolated color schemes. colour schemes can interpolated (diverging sequential data), colour range can limited additional argument. range allows remove fraction colour domain (interpolated; see examples).

title: Color Palette — colour
text: following palettes available. maximum number supported colours brackets, value relevant qualitative colour schemes (divergent sequential schemes linearly interpolated). Qualitative data bright (7), high contrast (3), vibrant (7), muted (9), medium contrast (6), pale (6), dark (6), light (9). Diverging data sunset (11), BuRd (9), PRGn (9). Sequential data YlOrBr (9), iridescent (23), discrete rainbow (23), smooth rainbow (34).

title: Color Palette — colour
text: According Paul Tol's technical note, bright, high contrast, vibrant muted colour schemes colourblind safe. medium contrast colour scheme designed situations needing colour pairs. light colour scheme reasonably distinct normal colourblind vision intended fill labeled cells. pale dark schemes distinct either normal colourblind vision used text background highlight cell table. Refer original document details recommended uses (see references).

title: Color Palette — colour
text: general rule, ordered data represented using rainbow scheme. three main arguments use (Tol 2018): spectral order visible light carries inherent magnitude message. bands almost constant hue sharp transitions , can perceived jumps data. Colour-blind people difficulty distinguishing colours rainbow. use avoided, Paul Tol's technical note provides two colour schemes reasonably clear colour-blind vision. remain colour-blind safe, two schemes must comply following conditions: discrete rainbow scheme must interpolated. smooth rainbow scheme used full range.
